task: Our shared workspace has agents launching runs while others try to observe them. I keep getting confused about which storage backends Smithers' DB adapter actually supports (openSmithersBackend / createSmithersPostgres). Name the three real backends so I can pin one and stop runs from being written to one store while my reads hit another.

Mid-project I added two fields to a Task's output Zod schema on top of a run history that already has finished nodes, retries, and approvals. I'm scared I have to delete smithers.db. On a SQLite-backed DB, which two SQL operations does Smithers run at boot to add the new columns in place without dropping data?
canonicalAnswer: ALTER TABLE, ADD COLUMN, IF NOT EXISTS

task: A long-running Loop produced ~2000 render frames. I assumed the frames table stores a full workflow-XML snapshot every single frame, which would be huge. How does Smithers actually keep the frames table compact, and what frame type does it write periodically so it can rebuild any frame N? Give the storage scheme and the interval.

I want to run an ad-hoc analytical query against a messy run DB (it has failed children, loop iterations, and tool calls) through the adapter's rawQuery helper. A teammate insists rawQuery will also let me DELETE stale rows and ALTER a table. Which statement kinds does rawQuery actually permit?

I'm opening smithers.db in sqlite3 to forensically inspect a stuck run, and I need to tell Smithers' own bookkeeping tables apart from my workflow's per-schema output tables. What prefix do all of Smithers' internal tables share, and what is the exact table that holds the top-level run records?

The monitor workflow wrote durable alerts into the DB during a long run. Before I script triage with `smithers alerts`, tell me the real, complete set of alert STATUSES a row can be in, plus the allowed SEVERITY levels, so I list/ack/resolve/silence correctly and don't guess names.
canonicalAnswer: firing, acknowledged, resolved, silenced, info, warning, critical

task: This repo has a legacy bun:sqlite smithers.db full of old runs, but the CLI now defaults to PGlite. `smithers up` launches and runs fine, yet `inspect`/`ps`/`output` HARD-FAIL with SMITHERS_MIGRATION_REQUIRED and my new run is invisible to them. Why does this happen, and what are the two correct ways to make the observe commands see the run?
notes: A correct answer must explain that `up` ran against SQLite while the read commands resolved to PGlite (the legacy store has run data but the resolved backend differs), so history is invisible until reconciled. The two fixes: (1) pin the backend with `backend: "sqlite" as const` in smithers.config.ts so ps/inspect/output/approve all use SQLite, or (2) run `smithers migrate` (default --to pglite, --keep-sqlite defaults true) to copy the legacy bun:sqlite history into PGlite and write the migrated.json marker. It must NOT recommend deleting/recreating smithers.db or hand-editing the DB, and should note the `--backend sqlite` CLI flag is not the fix.

task: Boot-time auto-migration happily ADDED my new output column on SQLite. But when I RENAMED a different field I still get `table output has no column named X`, and on our Postgres-backed deployment even adding a brand-new column fails. Explain why each case behaves differently and what the safe move is.
notes: A correct answer must state that boot-time migration only does CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS plus ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN (additive) and only on SQLite. Renaming, removing, or changing the type of a field is NOT auto-migrated and needs a fresh DB or a manual migration. Postgres does not auto-add columns yet, so new schema fields there require a migration/fresh run. It must not claim renames or type changes are handled automatically.

task: My Task output schema has a `z.boolean()` field `passed`. When I peek with `sqlite3 smithers.db "SELECT passed FROM ..."` I see 1 and 0, not true/false, yet inside my workflow ctx.output the value is a real JS boolean. What is happening under the hood, and do I need to change my schema?
notes: A correct answer must explain that SQLite has no native boolean type, so Smithers stores booleans as INTEGER 0/1; it records which columns are boolean and coerces 0/1 back to a real JS boolean when loading outputs/inputs. So raw sqlite3 shows 0/1 but the typed read coerces, and the schema needs no change. It must not claim SQLite stores native true/false or that you must switch to a numeric field.

task: I declared my Task's output Zod schema with fields `runId`, `iteration`, and `summary`, and now the persisted `iteration` value isn't what my code set — it tracks loop iteration instead. Which field names does the persistence layer reserve/auto-manage as key columns, and what should I do with my colliding field?
notes: A correct answer must identify runId, nodeId, and iteration as auto-managed key columns that Smithers strips from the payload and uses to key/alias the output snapshot row. You should NOT declare them in your output schema; rename the colliding field (e.g. iteration -> roundIndex). It must not advise keeping a user field named iteration/runId/nodeId.

task: I'm writing a custom compute Task that calls the DB adapter directly and want to wrap two writes in a transaction, but I'm already inside a Smithers-managed write. Are nested transactions supported? And with a Parallel fan-out hammering the same SQLite file, how does Smithers stop concurrent writes from corrupting each other?
notes: A correct answer must explain that writes are serialized through a per-database transaction queue (one turn at a time), and reads are gated by that same queue. Nested transactions are NOT supported and throw 'Nested sqlite transactions are not supported'; wrap your writes in a single withTransaction(writeGroup, op) instead of opening a transaction inside one. It must not claim nested BEGIN/SAVEPOINT works.

task: Under a heavy Parallel fan-out I intermittently see SQLITE_BUSY / 'database is locked' during persistence. Does Smithers retry these automatically? Does it retry forever, and does it treat a transient lock the same as a real error like a missing column?
notes: A correct answer must say Smithers wraps writes in a SQLite write-retry helper that retries ONLY transient/retryable errors (SQLITE_BUSY, SQLITE_IOERR, 'database is locked') using exponential backoff (base ~50ms), up to a bounded number of attempts (~10), then fails with DB_WRITE_FAILED. Non-retryable logical errors (e.g. missing column / schema mismatch) are NOT retried. It must not claim it retries every error or retries forever.

task: When I resume a run days later, how does Smithers know the on-disk database schema still matches the workflow's expected table layout, so it doesn't blindly write into a stale/incompatible set of tables? Where is the applied-migration state recorded, and what is compared?
notes: A correct answer must describe a computed schema signature (getSmithersSchemaSignature: a schemaVersion plus per-table component hashes) that is checked against the database catalog, with applied migrations tracked durably in the _smithers_schema_migrations table. The signature/catalog lets Smithers verify the DB matches the expected schema (and run-owned foreign keys are consistent) before writing. It must not claim Smithers just trusts the file or silently recreates everything.

task: For a forensic debug of a messy run (it has retries, two pending approvals, a WaitForEvent, and durable alerts), I'm querying smithers.db directly. Map each kind of durable state to its internal _smithers_* table: lifecycle events, durable signals a WaitForEvent consumes, approval decisions, durable human requests, per-attempt tool calls, frame snapshots, and durable alerts.
notes: A correct answer must map: lifecycle events -> _smithers_events; signals -> _smithers_signals; approval decisions -> _smithers_approvals; human requests -> _smithers_human_requests; tool calls -> _smithers_tool_calls; frame snapshots -> _smithers_frames; alerts -> _smithers_alerts (bonus: runs -> _smithers_runs, nodes -> _smithers_nodes, attempts -> _smithers_attempts). All internal tables share the _smithers_ prefix. Most table names must be correct, not invented.

task: Beyond runs/nodes/frames, which internal _smithers_* tables back these features: task-cache memoization, the Ralph loop ledger, scorer results, semantic vector embeddings, cached node diff bundles, registered cron schedules, sandbox tracking, the time-travel jump audit log, and the docs (tickets/plans/specs) store?
notes: A correct answer must map: cache -> _smithers_cache; ralph -> _smithers_ralph; scorers -> _smithers_scorers; vectors -> _smithers_vectors; node diffs -> _smithers_node_diffs; cron -> _smithers_cron; sandboxes -> _smithers_sandboxes; time-travel audit -> _smithers_time_travel_audit; docs -> _smithers_docs. Names must follow the _smithers_ prefix convention and be correct, not invented.

task: A flaky webhook calls `smithers signal deploy.completed --correlation prod-1 --data '{"sha":"abc"}'` twice with the byte-identical payload. The run has a WaitForEvent on that signal. Will it observe one delivery or two, how is each signal ordered/sequenced per run in the DB, and which fields can I filter persisted signals by?
notes: A correct answer must explain signals persist to _smithers_signals with an auto-allocated per-run sequence (MAX(seq)+1), and an identical signal (same run, signal name, correlation id, payload, timestamp, sender) is de-duplicated — the existing seq is returned and NO duplicate row is inserted, so the waiter sees one delivery. Signal queries can filter by signalName, correlationId, receivedAfterMs, and limit. It must not claim every signal call inserts a new row.

task: I'm building a live dashboard over `smithers events` for a long run with thousands of events across many nodes. How does the event history support incremental paging by sequence, filtering by node/type/time, getting a total count for a progress bar, and does it dedupe identical events? What allocates each event's sequence?
notes: A correct answer must describe events persisting to _smithers_events with an auto-allocated per-run seq; the history query supports afterSeq (incremental paging), nodeId, types, sinceTimestampMs, and limit; a count query returns the total; and identical events (same run, timestamp, type, payload) are deduplicated by returning the existing seq rather than inserting a duplicate. It may mention the queued events are flushed to the same DB. It must not invent a separate event store.

task: An agent in a paused run raised two durable `ask-human` requests. Walk me through how they're stored and the exact status lifecycle as an operator answers one and cancels the other, and how the pending inbox surfaces them with workflow/run context.
notes: A correct answer must say human requests persist to _smithers_human_requests with a status lifecycle of pending -> answered (answer stores the response value + answeredBy) OR pending -> cancelled OR pending -> expired (timeout); answering/cancelling only applies while pending; the pending-inbox query returns the pending requests joined with workflow name, run status, and node label so an operator knows what they're approving. It must not claim the statuses are approved/rejected.

task: I registered several `smithers cron` schedules, then disabled one and another started erroring. How are crons stored, how does the poller list only the enabled ones, where are the last-run and next-run times and the last error recorded, and what does `smithers cron rm` do to the row?
notes: A correct answer must explain crons persist to _smithers_cron; an enabled flag lets the poller list only enabled schedules (disabled rows remain stored, not deleted); last-run and next-run timestamps plus the last error are tracked on the row (last_run_at_ms / next_run_at_ms / error_json); and `cron rm` deletes that row by id. It must not claim disabling deletes the cron or that errors aren't persisted.

task: A detached run's process crashed and now shows `running` forever, while a healthy detached run nearby is just quiet between steps. How does Smithers tell the dead one is stale, atomically claim it for resume so two supervisors don't both grab it, and release the claim if it can't proceed? Which CLI surfaces this and how do I avoid killing the healthy run?
notes: A correct answer must explain that a run still marked running is classified stale by heartbeat staleness (no heartbeat within the threshold, ~30s) rather than by being idle; `smithers supervise` (and `smithers down`) find stale running runs and atomically claim them via a compare-and-set on the heartbeat/owner so only one supervisor wins, releasing the claim on failure; runs with fresh heartbeats are skipped by default (`down --force` overrides). It must not claim Smithers cancels any quiet run unconditionally or that there's no locking.

task: `smithers ps` shows a `status`, but `why`/inspect describe states like `stale` and `orphaned` that I never see stored. Which values are the actual persisted run statuses versus a derived classification, what inputs derive the state, and what specifically makes a run show up as `stale`?
notes: A correct answer must distinguish the fixed set of persisted run statuses (running, waiting-approval, waiting-event, waiting-timer, waiting-quota, finished, failed, cancelled, continued) from a DERIVED run-state view computed from the row plus pending approval/timer/event and heartbeat freshness, which adds classifications like stale and orphaned. A run is classified stale when its heartbeat is older than the threshold (~30s) while still marked running. It must not claim the stored terminal success status is 'succeeded' (it is 'finished').

task: `smithers diff` on a node with a giant DiffBundle feels like it recomputes every time, and I worry a huge diff could blow up memory. Does Smithers cache node diffs in the DB, what keys the cache entry, what stops an oversized diff from being cached, and is a concurrent recompute of the same node deduplicated?
notes: A correct answer must explain node diffs are cached in _smithers_node_diffs keyed by (run_id, node_id, iteration, base_ref); an oversized diff exceeding the byte limit (NODE_DIFF_MAX_BYTES) is rejected rather than cached (NodeDiffTooLargeError) to bound size; and concurrent in-flight computations of the same key are deduplicated so the diff is computed once and shared. It must not claim the cache is unbounded or has no size guard.

task: Before Smithers persists a workflow's input JSON or a Task's output, does it guard against pathological payloads — a million-element array, a deeply nested object, a multi-megabyte string? Which dimensions are bounded, and what else validates the payload shape?
notes: A correct answer must say yes: JSON bound helpers cap serialized byte size, nesting depth, string length, and array length before persistence (assertMaxBytes / assertMaxJsonDepth / assertMaxStringLength / assertOptionalArrayMaxLength), and the Zod input/output schemas (validateInput/validateOutput) validate the shape/types. It must not claim payloads are written to the DB unchecked.

task: On `--resume true`, where do a run's original input and each completed Task's prior outputs come from so finished work is skipped instead of re-run? Walk the load path and how a stored output row is matched back to its node.
notes: A correct answer must describe that input is loaded by run_id from the input table (loadInput) and each Task's output is loaded from its per-schema output table for the run, matched by the key columns runId/nodeId/iteration (the snapshot key aliasing / buildKeyWhere), with boolean columns coerced back. This persisted-output read is what lets resume skip completed tasks. It must not claim Smithers replays the agents to rebuild outputs or re-runs finished tasks.

task: When I run `smithers output --pretty` on a node row I get a schema-ordered, human-readable render instead of raw JSON key order. Conceptually, what does Smithers use to know the output table's shape and column order, and where was that shape defined?
notes: A correct answer must explain that the shape/column order comes from the output's registered Zod schema (passed to createSmithers, which becomes the SQLite table); Smithers can describe that schema shape (describeSchemaShape) to render columns in schema order. The output table is built from the Zod schema (payload-only, with the key columns auto-managed). It must not claim columns are ordered alphabetically or randomly.

task: Smithers keeps tickets/plans/specs in a docs table and a file watcher syncs them with files on disk. How is a doc stored and identified, what happens to the row when a doc is deleted, and how does reconcile handle a divergent edit where the file and the DB disagree?
notes: A correct answer must say docs persist to _smithers_docs keyed by path (with kind, content, content_hash, status, updated_at_ms); deletion is a SOFT delete (a deleted_at_ms tombstone), not a hard row drop, so it can be reconciled; and the file-watcher reconcile compares content hashes and writes a conflict marker when the file and DB diverge (e.g. a stale incoming update). It must not claim deletes hard-drop the row or that conflicts silently overwrite.

task: We want the Gateway and the CLI to share one Postgres so every agent sees the same runs. What does the Postgres dialect change versus SQLite — placeholders, types, the migration runner — and is the boot-time column auto-add supported on Postgres the way it is on SQLite?
notes: A correct answer must explain Smithers supports a Postgres dialect (createSmithersPostgres / backend postgres) whose layer translates SQLite SQL to Postgres: ? placeholders become $1.. and SQLite DDL/types are translated, and the same tracked _smithers_schema_migrations run via a Postgres migration runner against the schema catalog. Crucially, boot-time column auto-add is NOT yet supported on Postgres (only SQLite), so adding a schema field there needs a migration or fresh DB. It must not claim Postgres behaves identically to SQLite including auto-adding columns.

task: Author a Smithers workflow whose durable state survives a crash without redoing finished work. Use a <Loop> that runs a `build` <Task> (with an output Zod schema reference so its result is persisted to SQLite) up to 5 times until it succeeds, then a final `deploy` <Task> after the loop, also with an output schema. It must render via `smithers graph`.

Author a durable deploy workflow whose persisted state survives a process restart at any blocked point: a `build` <Task> with an output schema, then an <Approval> gate before production, then a <WaitForEvent> that blocks until a `deploy.completed` signal is delivered (via `smithers signal`), then a `verify` <Task>. Use only real components so it renders with `smithers graph`.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow><Task id="build" output={outputs.build} /><Approval id="prod-gate" /><WaitForEvent id="await-deploy" signal="deploy.completed" /><Task id="verify" output={outputs.verify} /></Workflow>

task: Author a workflow for a destructive data cleanup that needs durable human sign-off so the request survives a restart while it waits. Use a <HumanTask> that asks an operator to confirm, a <Timer> that bounds how long it waits, then a `delete` <Task> (with an output schema) that only runs after confirmation. It must render via `smithers graph`.

Author a workflow that isolates filesystem edits so each tool boundary can be snapshotted as a durable workspace checkpoint for later time-travel restore. Use a <Worktree> containing two code-editing <Task>s (each with an output schema). It must render via `smithers graph`.

Author a production-grade, maximally crash-safe durable release pipeline as ONE Smithers workflow. Requirements: isolate all work in a <Worktree>; fan out independent build and test agents with <Parallel> (each a <Task> with a typed output schema persisted to SQLite); gate production behind an <Approval>; block on an external <WaitForEvent> for the deploy webhook signal; and wrap the deploy step in a <Saga> so a failure triggers a compensating rollback. It must render via `smithers graph`.
canonicalAnswer: <Workflow><Worktree><Parallel><Task id="build" output={outputs.build} /><Task id="test" output={outputs.test} /></Parallel><Approval id="prod" /><WaitForEvent id="await-deploy" signal="deploy.completed" /><Saga><Task id="deploy" output={outputs.deploy} /></Saga></Worktree></Workflow>
